cards.q: List the general objectives of this lecture.
cards.a: Define immunization; significance of immunization; types of immunity; immunizing agents; national immunization schedule; contraindications to vaccinations; and reactions to vaccines.

cards.q: Define immunization.
cards.a: The procedure by which the body is prepared to fight against specific disease; it is used to induce the immune resistance of the body to a specific disease.

cards.q: What are the two types of immunization?
cards.a: Passive immunization and active immunization.

cards.q: How is passive immunization produced?
cards.a: Without challenging the immune system of the body — by administration of serum or gamma globulins from a person who is already immunized to a non-immune person.

cards.q: How is passive immunization acquired?
cards.a: Either naturally or artificially.

cards.q: How is passive natural immunization acquired before birth?
cards.a: Immunity is transferred from mother to fetus in the form of maternal antibodies — mainly IgG — through the placenta.

cards.q: How is passive natural immunization acquired after birth?
cards.a: Antibodies (IgA) are transferred through breast milk.

cards.q: How is passive artificial immunization developed, and what is it useful for?
cards.a: By injecting previously preformed antibodies using serum from humans or animals; useful for providing immediate protection against acute infections like tetanus and measles.

cards.q: How is active immunization acquired?
cards.a: By activating the immune system of the body — the body develops resistance by producing antibodies following exposure to antigens; acquired either naturally or artificially.

cards.q: What is active natural immunization?
cards.a: Activation of the immune system in the body to produce antibodies; achieved in both clinical and subclinical infections.

cards.q: What is active artificial immunization?
cards.a: Immunization achieved by the administration of vaccines or toxoids.

cards.q: Define a vaccine.
cards.a: A substance introduced into the body to prevent the disease produced by certain pathogens; it consists of dead pathogens or live but attenuated (artificially weakened) organisms.

cards.q: How does a vaccine induce immunity?
cards.a: Either by production of antibodies, or by activation of T-lymphocytes.

cards.q: Who produced the first live vaccine, and from what?
cards.a: Edward Jenner — he produced the vaccine for smallpox from cowpox virus.

cards.q: Define vaccination.
cards.a: The process of distributing and administering vaccine.

cards.q: What was the case fatality rate of smallpox, and what happened to survivors?
cards.a: 20–30% case fatality rates, leaving survivors scarred for life.

cards.q: When was variolation practised, and when did Edward Jenner publish?
cards.a: Variolation was practised as early as the 10th century AD; Edward Jenner was first to publish, in 1796.

cards.q: Who produced the rabies vaccine, and when?
cards.a: Louis Pasteur, 100 years after Jenner.

cards.q: Define a vaccine as an immunizing agent.
cards.a: Any preparation which, when administered, is able to stimulate the immune system to produce specific responses that inactivate, destroy or suppress a given pathogen.

cards.q: What are the two categories of vaccine?
cards.a: Live attenuated and inactivated.

cards.q: How are live attenuated vaccines produced?
cards.a: By modifying a naturally occurring (wild) organism, usually by repeated culturing.

cards.q: What are the properties of live attenuated vaccines?
cards.a: They retain the ability to replicate and produce an immune response similar to natural infection; circulating antibody may interfere with the response; they give long lasting immunity; and one dose usually suffices.

cards.q: Give examples of live attenuated VIRUSES.
cards.a: Poliomyelitis, measles, rubella, mumps, rabies, varicella, yellow fever, rotavirus, and influenza.

cards.q: Give examples of live attenuated BACTERIA.
cards.a: Bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) and typhoid.

cards.q: How are inactivated vaccines made?
cards.a: The organism is inactivated with heat and/or chemicals (formalin); with fractional vaccines it is further treated to purify only those components to be included in the vaccine.

cards.q: What are the properties of inactivated vaccines?
cards.a: Non-replicating so cannot cause disease; less interference from circulating antibodies; requires more doses (3–5); mostly humoral immune response; and antibody titres diminish with time.

cards.q: Give examples of inactivated VIRAL vaccines.
cards.a: Poliomyelitis (Salk), hepatitis A, rabies, and influenza.

cards.q: Give examples of inactivated BACTERIAL vaccines.
cards.a: Pertussis, typhoid, cholera, plague, and anthrax.

cards.q: What are subunit vaccines, and give examples.
cards.a: Fractional vaccines made from a single protein — hepatitis B, acellular pertussis, and human papilloma virus.

cards.q: Which vaccines are toxoids?
cards.a: Diphtheria and tetanus.

cards.q: Give examples of polysaccharide vaccines.
cards.a: Pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Salmonella typhi.

cards.q: Give examples of conjugate polysaccharide vaccines.
cards.a: Hib, pneumococcal, and meningococcal.

cards.q: Give examples of recombinant vaccines.
cards.a: Hepatitis B, human papilloma virus, and influenza — made from recombinant DNA technology.

cards.q: What is immune globulin, and what is it used for?
cards.a: Homologous pooled antibody from many adults, containing antibodies to many antigens — used for hepatitis A and measles.

cards.q: What is homologous human hyperimmune globulin, and what is it used for?
cards.a: Antibody from humans with high levels of specific antibodies — used for post-exposure prophylaxis of hepatitis B, rabies, tetanus and varicella.

cards.q: What is heterologous hyperimmune serum, and what is it used for?
cards.a: Antitoxin from animals — used for botulism and diphtheria.

cards.q: What are monoclonal antibodies, and give an example.
cards.a: Antibodies derived from a clone of antibody-producing cells — e.g. palivizumab for RSV.

cards.q: List the vaccine-related determinants of immune response.
cards.a: Chemical and physical state of the vaccine; route/site of administration; vaccine dose; and immunogenicity of the vaccine.

cards.q: List the host factors determining immune response.
cards.a: Age, sex, nutrition, pre-existing antibodies, genetics, immune status, and pregnancy.

cards.q: What do antitoxins do?
cards.a: Inactivate soluble toxic protein products.

cards.q: What do opsonins do?
cards.a: Facilitate phagocytosis.

cards.q: What do lysins do?
cards.a: Interact with components of serum complement.

cards.q: What do neutralising antibodies do?
cards.a: Prevent proliferation of infectious virus.

cards.q: What do antiadhesins do?
cards.a: Prevent adhesion to mucosal surfaces.

cards.q: List the factors to consider in vaccine recommendation.
cards.a: Epidemiology (incidence, age, sex); age-specific morbidity and mortality; risk of adverse events; cost effectiveness; age of recommended health care visits; and efficacy of the vaccine.

cards.q: At what ages are vaccinations generally recommended?
cards.a: At the age of significant risk of disease and complications, and at the age of protective immunologic response.

cards.q: List the objectives of the immunization programme.
cards.a: Not just individual protection; to decrease morbidity and mortality from target diseases; and to increase herd immunity — which requires high coverage.

cards.q: Define herd immunity.
cards.a: The resistance of a group to invasion and spread of an infectious agent, due to immunity of a large proportion of the group.

cards.q: List the methods of vaccine delivery.
cards.a: Routine immunization; response to an epidemic; supplemental immunization activities; mop up immunization; and mobile or fixed locations.

cards.q: In which conditions is pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine indicated?
cards.a: Sickle cell anaemia, anatomic asplenia, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure, HIV, and organ transplant.

cards.q: Which vaccines may be given in HIV, and which are contraindicated?
cards.a: All vaccines if asymptomatic; BCG and yellow fever are contraindicated if symptomatic; and IPV is preferred to OPV.

cards.q: What is the general rule about live vaccines in immunocompromised children?
cards.a: Live vaccines are generally not given to immunocompromised children.

cards.q: List the contraindications to vaccination.
cards.a: Anaphylaxis; encephalopathy; progressive neurologic disorders; immunodeficiency disorder and immunosuppressive therapy; pregnancy; and any illness requiring admission to hospital — defer immunization.

cards.q: List the precautions in vaccination.
cards.a: Fever above 40.5 °C; collapse or shock; inconsolable crying; Guillain–Barré syndrome; and for MMR, blood transfusion and thrombocytopenia.

cards.q: List the things that are NOT contraindications to vaccination.
cards.a: Upper respiratory tract infection; mild fever; diarrhoea (but repeat the dose); cerebral palsy; breastfeeding; and antimicrobial therapy.

cards.q: List the adverse reactions to vaccines.
cards.a: Pain, swelling, redness and soreness; fever; anaphylaxis; encephalopathy (convulsions, coma); and rash or urticaria.

cards.q: List the elements of cost effectiveness of immunization.
cards.a: Savings on treatment cost; decreased mortality; decreased morbidity and disability; avoidance of intangible costs (suffering); and external or spillover benefits.

cards.q: How much did smallpox eradication save?
cards.a: 32 billion dollars.

cards.q: Define immunization coverage, and in which age group is it often evaluated?
cards.a: The percentage of the target population that have received immunization; often evaluated in children aged 12–23 months, which estimates recent immunization performance.

cards.q: Define a missed opportunity.
cards.a: When an individual in whom there is no contraindication does not receive the vaccine(s) for which he or she is eligible, when he or she visits a health facility; it can occur during curative or preventive visits.

cards.q: List the causes of missed opportunities.
cards.a: Non-availability of vaccine; not checking immune status; failure to give simultaneous injections; failure to provide information to parents; and failure to immunize every day.

cards.q: Define the cold chain, and why is it necessary?
cards.a: A system of storage and transport of vaccines at low temperatures by the manufacturer to the actual vaccination site; necessary because vaccine failure may occur due to failure of storage and transport under strict temperature controls.

cards.q: Which vaccines are stored in the freezer compartment?
cards.a: Polio and measles.

cards.q: Which vaccines must be stored in the cold compartment and never allowed to freeze?
cards.a: BCG, DPT, DT, TT, typhoid, and diluents.

cards.q: What two things must vaccines be protected from?
cards.a: Sunlight, and contact with antiseptics.

cards.q: How long can most vaccines be stored at health centres, and at what temperature?
cards.a: Up to 5 weeks (except polio), if the refrigerator temperature is strictly kept between 4 °C and 8 °C.

cards.q: How long may an opened vial be stored, with and without preservatives?
cards.a: With preservatives, up to 3 hours; without preservatives, up to 1 hour.

cards.q: List the cold chain equipment.
cards.a: Walk-in cold rooms, deep freezer, small deep freezer, cold boxes, vaccine carrier, day carrier, and ice packs.
